source: Agent 13 — Children's author, Rhema debate April 2026

stories.title: Ruth Picks Naomi
stories.story: Naomi lost her husband. Then she lost her two sons. She was old, tired, and far from home. She told her daughters-in-law, "Go back to your families. Start a new life." One girl hugged her and left. But Ruth held on tight. "Wherever you go, I will go," Ruth said. "Your God will be my God." They walked the long road to Bethlehem with empty pockets. To eat, Ruth picked up leftover grain in the fields. A kind man named Boaz owned that field. He saw Ruth working hard for Naomi. He told his workers, "Leave extra grain for her." Boaz and Ruth fell in love. They got married. Soon Ruth had a baby boy named Obed. Naomi held the baby and smiled. Her arms were full again. That baby grew up. He had a son named Jesse. Jesse had a son named David, who became king. And from David's family, one day, came Jesus. Ruth stayed when it was hard. God turned her loyalty into a family tree that reached all the way to the Savior.
stories.keyVerse: Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay. Your people will be my people and your God my God. — Ruth 1:16
stories.oneQuestion: When is it hard to stay loyal to someone you love?
stories.pictureCue: Ruth and Naomi walking a dusty road at sunset, hand in hand, with Bethlehem on the horizon.

theme.music: Low cello with gentle acoustic guitar melody growing fuller
theme.voice: Warm, steady woman's voice — like a grandmother telling a bedtime truth

stories.title: The Roof Came Off
stories.story: Jesus was teaching inside a small house. The house was packed. People crammed the doorway. More people stood outside on tiptoe. Four friends came carrying a man on a mat. His legs did not work. They had to get him to Jesus. But they could not even squeeze through the door. The friends looked up. The roof was flat and made of mud and sticks. One friend grinned. "Let's go up." They carried their friend up the outside stairs. They started digging. Clump. Crack. Dust fell into the house. A hole got bigger and bigger. People inside looked up with wide eyes. Then down came the mat, slowly, on ropes, right in front of Jesus. Jesus looked up at the four friends. He smiled at their faith. He told the man on the mat, "Your sins are forgiven. Stand up. Pick up your mat. Walk home." The man wiggled his toes. He stood. He rolled up his mat. He walked out the door, strong and tall. The crowd gasped. The four friends on the roof cheered. Jesus can fix what nobody else can. And good friends don't give up.
stories.keyVerse: When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralyzed man, 'Son, your sins are forgiven.' — Mark 2:5
stories.oneQuestion: Who is one friend you would carry to Jesus?
stories.pictureCue: Four friends lowering a man on a mat through a hole in the roof while Jesus looks up and smiles below.

theme.music: Percussive hand drums and tambourine crescendoing into triumphant brass at the roof break
theme.voice: Four kid voices on action lines, one calm adult voice for Jesus

stories.title: The Axe That Floated
stories.story: Elisha was a prophet of God. A group of young men followed him and learned from him. Their house got too small. "Let's build a bigger one by the river," they said. Elisha agreed and came with them. They grabbed axes and started chopping down trees. Chop. Chop. Chop. One young man swung his axe hard. WHOOSH. The iron axe head flew off the wooden handle. SPLASH. It sank straight into the deep river. The young man groaned. "Oh no! That axe wasn't mine. I borrowed it!" He stared at the water. Axe heads are heavy. He would never get it back. Elisha walked over. "Where did it fall?" The young man pointed. Elisha picked up a stick. He tossed it into the river, right where the axe had dropped. The young man watched. Suddenly, the iron axe head bobbed up to the top. It floated. Floated! Iron does not float. But nothing is too small or too hard for God. "Pick it up," Elisha said. The young man reached out and grabbed it, dripping wet. God cares about big troubles. God also cares about a borrowed tool and one worried helper.
stories.keyVerse: The man of God said, 'Where did it fall?' When he showed him the place, Elisha cut a stick and threw it there, and made the iron float. — 2 Kings 6:6
stories.oneQuestion: What small worry can you tell God about today?
stories.pictureCue: A wet iron axe head bobbing on river ripples, a stick floating beside it, a surprised young worker reaching down.

theme.music: Sparse acoustic plucks with a single bright chime on the miracle moment
theme.voice: Curious, boyish narrator — whispers the suspense, grins at the splash

stories.title: The Chariot and the Question
stories.story: Philip was walking down a desert road. An angel had told him, "Go south." Philip did not ask why. He just went. Soon he saw a fancy chariot rolling by. Inside sat an important man from Ethiopia. He worked for the queen. He took care of all her money. He was coming home from Jerusalem. He had a scroll on his lap. He was reading out loud as the horses clopped along. God's Spirit whispered to Philip, "Run. Catch that chariot." Philip ran. He heard the man reading words from the prophet Isaiah. Words about a lamb who would be hurt to save people. Philip called up, "Do you understand what you're reading?" The man shook his head. "How can I, unless someone explains it?" He invited Philip to hop up and sit beside him. Bump, bump, bump. Philip told him all about Jesus. The man's eyes grew bright. They came to some water by the road. "Look! Water!" the man said. "Can I be baptized right now?" They stopped the chariot. They waded in. Philip baptized him. The Ethiopian climbed out laughing, full of joy. He drove home to a whole new country with good news to share.
stories.keyVerse: How can I understand, unless someone explains it to me? — Acts 8:31
stories.oneQuestion: Who could you explain Jesus to, even just a little?
stories.pictureCue: A dusty road, a golden chariot with horses, Philip and the Ethiopian official waist-deep in a roadside pool of water.

theme.music: Travel-rhythm African hand drums plus a lone reed flute opening into joyful choir harmonies at baptism
theme.voice: Two narrators trading lines — one for Philip, one for the official — ending in warm laughter

stories.title: Lydia and the Purple Cloth
stories.story: Lydia sold purple cloth. Purple was the fanciest color in the world. Kings wore it. Rich ladies wore it. Lydia's cloth was beautiful, and her shop did well. She lived in a city called Philippi. Every Sabbath, Lydia walked down to the river with other women. They sat on the grass and prayed to God together. They did not have a building. The riverbank was their church. One Sabbath, a stranger named Paul sat down with them. He had sailed a long way to get there. He started telling them about Jesus. Lydia listened with her whole heart. The Bible says God opened her heart, like a door swinging wide. She believed every word. Right there at the river, Lydia and her whole family were baptized. Water dripped from their chins. They were new. Lydia looked at Paul and his friends. They had no place to stay. "Come to my house," she said. "If you believe I really follow Jesus, stay with me." She would not take no for an answer. Paul stayed. Her big house became the first church in Europe. A purple-cloth seller opened her door, and a whole continent started hearing about Jesus.
stories.keyVerse: The Lord opened her heart to respond to Paul's message. — Acts 16:14
stories.oneQuestion: How can you open your home or lunch table for someone?
stories.pictureCue: Lydia in a purple shawl on a grassy riverbank, dripping with river water, welcoming Paul toward her open front door.

theme.music: Mediterranean string quartet (violin, mandolin) with soft water sounds under the riverbank scene
theme.voice: Confident, welcoming adult woman's voice, slightly lower — smile audible on 'Come'
